durable / execution on NATS
Not a server How it works Correctness The UI GitHub ↗
Durable execution · on NATS · a library, not a server

@synadia-ai/durable

Crash-proof workflows — with no new server to run.

Write workflows as ordinary async functions. Every side-effecting or nondeterministic step is journaled to an ordered log and replayed after a crash instead of being re-executed. The NATS cluster you already run is the engine.

In one lineThere is no durable-execution server and no separate database — JetStream streams + KV are the journal, the work queue, the timers, and the status index. Your only moving parts are NATS and your own workers.
v0.1 · feature-complete 107 tests green pure-core + real-NATS proof dual ESM + CJS wire format frozen at v:1 Apache-2.0

01 — The problem

A crash in the middle of a function is where correctness goes to die.

Picture a function that reserves inventory, charges a card, and ships an order.

If the process dies after the charge but before the ship, your two instincts both fail. A naïve retry runs the whole thing again and double-charges. A no-retry policy loses the order. So you reach for the usual scaffolding: hand-rolled state machines, status columns, idempotency tables, outbox patterns — per workflow, forever. That scaffolding is the problem durable execution exists to delete.

02 — It’s not what you think

Durable execution usually means a server. This isn’t that.

Most durable-execution systems ship a dedicated control plane — a service that stores workflow state, holds the queues and timers, drives execution, and is backed by its own database. You run it, scale it, secure it, patch it.

We don’t have one. The NATS cluster is the engine. This is a library that runs inside your own processes and uses JetStream streams and KV — things NATS already does — as the durable substrate. Three assumptions to drop right away:

Not a workflow server

No control plane

Nothing to deploy, scale, secure, or pay for. Your processes plus NATS are all there is.

Not memory magic

No stack snapshot

It doesn’t freeze your process or heap. It re-runs the function from the top and replays journaled ops.

Not a new database

No Postgres

No bespoke store to back it. JetStream streams and KV are the entire durable substrate.

“No server” doesn’t mean “no infrastructure” — NATS is infrastructure. It means no new, durable-execution-specific service. If you already run NATS, you already run the engine.

03 — The core trick

Journal, then replay.

A workflow is an ordinary async function over a ctx. Every step that touches the outside world or is otherwise nondeterministic goes through ctx — and ctx writes the step’s result to an ordered journal before returning it.

When a worker (re)starts a run it does not resume a paused coroutine. It simply re-runs the function from the top — but now each ctx.* call first consults the journal: already recorded → return the saved value, don’t run it again (that’s replay); not yet recorded → this is the live frontier: run it for real, record the result, continue.

The one rule this asks of you: everything nondeterministic goes through ctx — no bare Date.now(), Math.random(), or I/O in the workflow body. The boundary is easy to see (it’s literally the ctx. prefix), and the runtime fails loud if replay ever diverges.

04 — The moving parts

A small, fixed footprint on NATS — and that’s the whole engine.

Four NATS resources do all the work. Your workers and clients hold no durable state of their own; they are just the library, reading and writing these.

05 — What you get

The contract, stated plainly.

Three guarantees fall out of the design. The first two are the standard durable-execution contract; the third is what keeps a subtle bug from silently corrupting a run.

State

Exactly-once

Every step is journaled exactly once; replay returns the recorded value and never re-runs the function. The run’s result is deterministic no matter how many workers touched it.

Effects

At-least-once

A step’s function can run more than once across its begin→end window (a crash, an ack-timeout redelivery, or a second worker on a concurrent wake). ctx.key() = {run}:{seq} makes the effect idempotent.

Divergence

Fail loud

If a replay ever produces a different step than the journal recorded — a changed name, a changed kind, a shorter path — the run aborts with NonDeterminismError instead of corrupting silently.

Exactly-once state, at-least-once effects — the standard durable-execution contract. The difference here is what implements it: not a coordinator, but an append.

06 — Why it’s correct

One primitive. One error code.

Everything that must be correct under concurrency reduces to a single NATS capability: the optimistic append.

When you publish to a run’s journal subject, you can say “accept this only if the subject’s last entry is still sequence S.” If someone else already appended, the server rejects you with one specific error — code 10071, “wrong last sequence” — which the library maps to FencedError. KV create / update(rev) is the same primitive (KV is a stream). Progress is never gated on a plain read.

Why “two-phase”? A step writes its intent (step-begin) before running fn, then writes the result (step-end). A fresh duplicate loses the begin CAS and stops before any effect; a resumed mid-step duplicate may re-run fn, but only one step-end wins and the loser adopts the recorded value. That is precisely “exactly-once state, at-least-once effects.” Two free safety nets ride along: message dedup (Nats-Msg-Id) catches an identical redelivered append, and fail-loud replay guards catch nondeterministic code.

And the failure modes are separated on purpose: a transient infra error (a publish timeout, a failed schedule) makes the worker NAK and retry — it never fails your run; only genuine workflow-logic errors do.

07 — The life of a run

From start to result, step by step.

1
Start. client.start appends a start entry to DE_JOURNAL (idempotent on the runId) and drops a work item on DE_WORK.
2
Pull. A free worker pulls the item and loads the run’s journal from DE_JOURNAL.
3
Replay & advance. It re-runs the workflow from the top — journaled steps return saved values; the first new step is the frontier: it runs, appends its result (CAS), advances.
4
Finish — or yield. Reached the end → append result, update DE_RUNS, ack. Hit a sleep/await → suspend; a wake re-enqueues a work item later. Worker died → the item is redelivered and another worker resumes (back to 2).
5
Observe. handle.result() resolves when the journal gets its terminal entry — surviving worker restarts in between.

Because step 3 always rebuilds state from the journal, a crash anywhere is just “go back to step 2.”

08 — The artifact

Your code, on the wire, in the UI — same bytes, three readers.

There is no hidden state record. A run is an append-only log of JSON entries on de.journal.{runId}. Here is one workflow, the exact entries it produces, and the dashboard’s view of those same entries — all the checkout-ok run.

The full ctx surface — and the NATS feature behind each

You writeYou getThe NATS feature behind it
ctx.run(name, fn)a durable stepappend to the JetStream journal (two-phase, CAS-fenced)
ctx.now / random / uuid()deterministic valuesjournaled value-capture (a stream append)
ctx.sleep(name, ms)a durable timernative JetStream message scheduling
ctx.await(name, {timeoutMs?})wait for a signal (opt. timeout)a journaled signal in arrival order; a timeout races a durable timer
ctx.call(wf, input)invoke a sub-workflowa child run + a work-queue item
ctx.continueAsNew(input)restart fresh; bound the journala continued terminal → a new generation
ctx.key()an idempotency keyderived from runId:stepSeq
client.start / result / statuslaunch & observe a runstream append (start) + reading the journal / KV
a workerruns your workflow codea JetStream pull consumer (queue group)
09 — The proof

Correctness you can re-run.

107
tests green
2
layers — pure core + real NATS
3
latent bugs found & fixed
6
signals in the chaos pass

The replay engine depends only on a Journal interface, so the entire crash / replay / concurrency story runs in-memory against an InMemoryJournal — a NATS-faithful double with the same CAS-dominates-dedup ordering and the same codec round-trip. The second layer drives full worker + client runs against a spawned nats-server -js (and skips cleanly if the binary is absent), pinning the load-bearing primitives directly. The headline result: a two-worker, six-concurrent-signal chaos pass proving exactly-once journaling, at-least-once effects, and CAS-dominates-dedup on a real server.

The test bias is adversarial — every feature is probed for its crash / fencing / divergence edges, not just the happy path. A topic-by-topic coverage audit then found and fixed three latent bugs, each pinned by a regression test verified to fail without its fix:

A1 · FIFO

await-timeout

A timed-out await wrongly consumed a signal slot, so the natural “await with timeout, catch, loop” pattern then matched the wrong signal. Fixed with single-pass resolution.

A2 · encode

fail, not loop

A non-serializable return was misclassified as transient infra error → NAK-loop forever. Now a SerializationError fails the run terminally. (Hidden because the in-memory double never encoded — it now round-trips the codec.)

A3 · nesting

step guard

A ctx.* call inside a step fn interleaved sequence numbers — it worked once, then threw NonDeterminismError on every replay. Now a StepNestingError fails fast and identically.

10 — See it live

One dashboard, reading runs straight from NATS.

The observability UI (Vue 3 + a Bun/Hono backend, live over SSE) decodes the same journal you just read — no extra store, no agent. It is the “what we built”, made tangible.

11 — The honest edges

What we haven’t built — on purpose, and on the record.

The correctness core holds today, but this is not yet a production runtime. The candid list of gaps, none of which compromise the guarantees above:

no gating KV lease

Correctness holds without one — the append CAS is the guard. A lease would only narrow the at-least-once window (cut duplicate step execution) under heavy concurrent multi-worker load.

no cancellation

There is no client.cancel. Once started, a run — and any ctx.call children it spawned — runs to completion or failure. It can’t be stopped.

no in-flight versioning

A wfVersion is carried on the journal, but running workflows aren’t patched mid-flight.

no >8 MiB payloads

Bounded by the NATS max payload; there’s no object-store offload for large step values yet.

12 — Why this way

Why NATS makes this uniquely possible.

The same primitive — an optimistic append — is the journal’s single-writer fence, the KV’s compare-and-set, and (via native scheduling and work-queue streams) the timer and the queue. One capability, reused everywhere. That’s why it can be a library instead of a server:

Operate

Nothing new to run

No DE server to deploy, scale, patch, secure, or pay for — and no separate database. Workers are stateless; all durability lives in NATS.

Inherit

Rides your existing NATS

Multi-region, clustering, auth, back-pressure, and observability are whatever your NATS already provides.

Embed

It’s a library

No network hop to a control plane to start a workflow. start is an append; a worker is a consumer.

Port

Language-neutral by design

The on-stream format is frozen at v:1 and specified normatively, so Go / Python / … workers can share the same runs.

NATS is the engine.

Status — v0.1, feature-complete, not yet published. The full ctx surface, the worker/client loop, two-phase journaling and crash-replay are implemented and tested (107 green). Production gaps are tracked above, honestly.

@synadia-ai/durable · durable execution on NATS / JetStream · Apache-2.0
